content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 22, 1992

Kristina Veaco
Attorney
Legal Department
Pacific Telesis
Pacific Telesis Center
130 Kearny Street, Room 3609
San Francisco, CA  94108

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-92-533

Dear Ms. Veaco:

     This is in reply to your request for advice on behalf of
Pacific Telesis Group ("PTG") concerning whether certain
activities by PTG constitute reportable contributions, independent
expenditures or expenditures under the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1

QUESTION

     PTG makes regular communications to its employees by media
other than printed newsletters. These communications may sometimes
contain political information but are generally used to
communicate non-political information.  Is PTG's use of funds to
make these communications a contribution, independent expenditure
or expenditure under the Act?

CONCLUSION

     PTG's use of funds to make these communications to employees
is not a contribution, independent expenditure or expenditure
under the Act.


1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95812  •  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-92-533
Page 2


FACTS

PTG uses various media to communicate with its 60,000
employees on a regular basis. Generally, the communications
contain information relevant to the Company and come from internal
as well as external sources. Messages could include discussions
of products, legislation affecting the Company, or the Company's
financial performance. Generally, the messages are not for
political purposes.

In addition to regularly published printed newsletters
distributed to employees, there are two daily voicemails. The
"Telesis Media Report" is sent automatically to approximately 500
subscribing employees, and the "Pacific Bell Media Report" to
approximately 1,800 subscribing employees. Messages generally do
not exceed 2-3 minutes, but could run as long as 5 minutes. An
electronic mail newsletter, "Fastlink," is published daily and
distributed to approximately 20,000 Pacific Bell employees.

Fastlink averages between 9-12 computer screens in length.
Additionally, Pacific Bell has established an 800 number for
employees to call to receive "Newsbeat," recorded regularly.
Messages generally do not exceed 2 minutes, but could run as long
as 5 minutes. Newsbeat averages 800 callers per day.

Pacific Bell is in the process of establishing a custom 800
telephone number for employees to call to obtain information on a
number of subjects ranging from product information to a daily
media report. Also contemplated is a section called "Legislative
Update" that will provide the status of pending legislation either
at the state or federal level. When an employee calls, he/she
will be able to choose a subject from a menu. Messages do not
exceed 3 minutes in length. As in the case of the other PTG
newsletters, generally this newsletter will not be used for
political purposes.

Each newsletter described above has established procedures as
to the length of the publication, broadcast, or message, and the
regularity of publication.

In connection with the upcoming elections, PTG is planning to
communicate its position on various state ballot measures to its
employees using some or all of the newsletters described above.

DISCUSSION

If an organization such as PTG makes contributions of $10,000
or more, or independent expenditures of $1,000 or more, in a
calendar year, it qualifies as a committee under the Act.
(Section 82013.)

Our File No. A-92-533
Page 3


Once an individual or organization qualifies as a committee,
it is required to file various types of statements and reports,
many of which require disclosure of the contributions, independent
expenditures and expenditures the committee makes. (See generally
Sections 84100-84226.) Furthermore, contributions made to
candidates in special elections, or in some local jurisdictions to
all candidates, are subject to dollar limits within specified time
periods. (See Section 85305.)

Section 82015 includes within the definition of contribution
"[a]n expenditure made at the behest of a candidate, committee or
elected officer ...." Regulation 18215(b) defines "made at the
behest" as "a payment made under the control or at the direction
of, in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with,
or at the request or suggestion of a candidate, controlled
committee, official committee of a political party, or
organization formed or existing primarily for political purposes."

Section 82031 defines "independent expenditure" as an
expenditure "in connection with a communication which expressly
advocates the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate
or the qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly identified
issue ... which is not made to or at the behest of the affected
candidate or committee."

Section 82025 defines "expenditure" essentially as a payment
that is made for political purposes.

Based on these definitions alone, it is possible that PTG,
when expending funds to make the various communications which
include political information to its employees described above,
could be making contributions, independent expenditures, or
expenditures under the Act. However, the Commission has, by
regulation and written advice, made exceptions to these
definitions.

Regulation 18225(b)(4)(C) states that the term "expenditure"
does not include

... costs incurred for communications
which expressly advocate the nomi-
nation, election or defeat of a clearly
identified candidate or candidates or
the qualification, passage or defeat

Our File No. A-92-533
Page 4


                    of a clearly identified measure or
                    measures by:

                         (C) A regularly published news-
                    letter or regularly published
                    periodical ... whose circulation
                    is limited to an organization's
                    members, employees, shareholders,
                    other affiliated individuals and
                    those who request or purchase
                    the publication. This paragraph
                    applies only to the costs re-
                    gularly incurred in publishing
                    and distributing the newsletter
                    or periodical. If additional
                    costs are incurred because the
                    newsletter is issued on other than
                    its regular schedule, expanded in
                    circulation, or substantially
                    altered in style, size or format,
                    the additional costs are expendi-
                    tures.

     Based upon this provision, the Commission has excepted the
costs for regularly published employee newsletters from the Act's
definition of expenditures and independent expenditure even if the
newsletter contains information which would ordinarily make the
cost an expenditure or independent expenditure.

     Furthermore, the Commission has extended this exception to
apply to the costs for similarly published employee newsletters,
even if the newsletter contains information which would ordinarily
constitute the making of a contribution to a candidate or
committee. (See Lowell Advice Letter, No. A-92-475.)

     Therefore, the question you present is whether the various
communications described above are employee newsletters within
the definition of Regulation 18225(b)(4)(C).

     Your question concerns five different communications, each of
which is or will be from PFI and exclusively for its employees.
Four of these communications, "Telesis Media Report," "Pacific
Bell Media Report," "Newsbeat," and a contemplated call-in 800
number, are by telephone. A fifth, "Fastlink," is by computer
electronic mail. The four existing communications, "Telesis Media
Report," "Pacific Bell Media Report," "Fastlink," and "Newsbeat"
are communicated on a regular basis and have established
approximate lengths and formats. The contemplated call-in 800
number is also planned to operate on a daily basis with a standard
approximate length and format.

Our File No. A-92-533
Page 5


     Based on these facts, PTG's costs in making these
communications are not contributions, independent expenditures, or
expenditures under the act.  Each of these communications falls
within the parameters of the exception stated in Regulation
18225(b)(4)(C) and differ from previous exempted communications
by Commission advice letter because they are not in printed form.
However, the regulation does not require that the newsletter be
"printed."  Rather, it requires that the newsletter be regularly
"published."  The term "publish" primarily means to make publicly
known; announce, proclaim, divulge, or promulgate."  (Websters New
World Dict. [2d college ed., 1974] p. 1149; also see Black's Law
Dict. [rev. 4th ed., 1968] p. 1397.) The PTG communications in
question meet this definition and, therefore, are within the
regulation's exception.

     Should you have any questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Scott Hallabedin
                                        Acting General Counsel

SH:dl

other than printed newsletter.
These communications may sometimes

Is PTG’s use of funds to
